advisory.checklist_item: - [ ] Restore analyzer_module after the reload (reload again in a finalizer, or use monkeypatch to swap ANALYZERS instead of importlib.reload) so the heldout test does not leak module state into the session.
evidence: `importlib.reload(analyzer_module)` with no fixture/finalizer restoring the original module state

test_analyzer.py imports the name: `from rebar.metrics.analyzer import (ANALYZERS, ...)` and mutates it via `monkeypatch.setitem(ANALYZERS, "python", configured)`, then asserts `resolve_analyzer("python") is configured`
analyzer.py resolve_analyzer resolves the module global at call time: `return ANALYZERS.get(language)`; after reload the module global is a NEW dict distinct from the name test_analyzer.py mutated

No pytest-randomly plugin is configured (no 'randomly' in pyproject.toml), so default alphabetical order (test_analyzer.py before test_analyzer_heldout.py) masks the failure today, making it a latent rather than active bug
advisory.finding: test_analyzer_import_is_additive_and_unsupported_signals_default_to_none calls importlib.reload(analyzer_module) but never restores the module afterward, leaking a rebound ANALYZERS dict into the rest of the pytest session; combined with test_analyzer.py binding ANALYZERS by name at import time while resolve_analyzer reads the module global, this is an order-dependent test-isolation hazard.
scenarios: Any future re-ordering of collection (e.g. adding pytest-randomly, running a single -k selection that inverts order, or splitting across xdist workers) makes resolve_analyzer consult the reloaded empty dict and fails the happy-path assertion
Subsequent tests added to tests/unit/metrics that touch analyzer_module.ANALYZERS inherit the reloaded module state
advisory.suggested_fix: Replace the unrestored importlib.reload with a monkeypatch.setattr(analyzer_module, "ANALYZERS", {}) (or a fresh submodule import into a throwaway name), or register a finalizer that reloads analyzer_module a second time to restore its original ANALYZERS binding after the assertion.

coverage.approach_viability_note: 0 surviving high-priority finding(s), Pass-2 drop-rate 50% — the approach (not just nits) may be worth a second look (advisory; the verdict is unchanged).

dropped.checklist_item: - [ ] Restore the reloaded `rebar.metrics.analyzer` module after `importlib.reload` (fixture/finalizer) so the test cannot leak a divergent module instance into later tests.
evidence: `importlib.reload(analyzer_module)` is invoked inside `test_analyzer_import_is_additive_and_unsupported_signals_default_to_none` with no fixture/finalizer restoring the module

`from rebar.metrics.analyzer import analyze_or_unavailable, resolve_analyzer` (test_analyzer_heldout.py:10) and the same by-name imports in test_analyzer.py bind to the pre-reload module objects, which the reload replaces globally
ABSENCE: no `addfinalizer`, fixture, or `monkeypatch`-based restoration of `sys.modules['rebar.metrics.analyzer']` after the reload
dropped.finding: The additive-import test calls `importlib.reload(analyzer_module)` with no teardown to restore the module, leaking a reloaded module object into the rest of the pytest session; by-value imports of `analyze_or_unavailable`/`resolve_analyzer`/`ANALYZERS` elsewhere can bind to a different module instance than `analyzer_module`, creating an order-dependent isolation hazard.
dropped.suggested_fix: Wrap the reload in a fixture that snapshots `sys.modules['rebar.metrics.analyzer']` (and reloads once more, or restores it) in teardown; or assert additivity by checking `REGISTRY` is unchanged after a fresh `importlib.import_module` in a subprocess / without mutating the shared module.
